critic_raw:
The split strategy uses stratified random sampling across a longitudinal diabetic retinopathy cohort where patients have both a baseline and at least one follow-up assessment 12+ months later. Stratifying randomly by follow-up interval and imaging facility does not prevent the same patient from appearing in both the training and test sets. With 10,000 patient records over 5 years and a requirement for baseline and follow-up imaging, some patients will have multiple time points. If a patient's baseline-to-12-month pair appears in training and their baseline-to-24-month pair appears in test, the model encounters the same patient in both splits. This is patient-level leakage — the model may learn patient-specific anatomical features that predict their personally-observed trajectory rather than generalizable progression risk.

All normalization and scaling transformers are 'fit on the full dataset before splitting to ensure stability.' For retinal fundus photographs with known facility-to-facility imaging variation, fitting normalization on all images before splitting means the per-channel means and standard deviations used to standardize test images are partially derived from those test images themselves. In a study with 3+ imaging facilities, this artificially reduces the inter-facility distribution shift that the model would face in real deployment — where new patient images from a previously-unseen facility would need to be standardized using only training-set statistics.

Progression is defined as 'advancement to a more severe ETDRS stage on follow-up imaging, adjudicated by board-certified ophthalmologists blinded to baseline clinical data.' This blinding creates an asymmetric information condition: ophthalmologists grading follow-up images are blind to baseline EHR data, but the model is trained to predict progression using baseline EHR features. If the ophthalmologist grading is done blinded to EHR information, the labels capture imaging-based progression as judged without clinical context — while the model uses clinical context (HbA1c, blood pressure, medications) to predict those grades. The labels and the model inputs operate under different information sets, potentially creating label-feature incoherence.

Macro-averaged F1 as the primary metric for a binary prediction task (progression vs. no progression) is unusual and potentially misleading. For binary classification, macro F1 is simply the average of the positive-class F1 and negative-class F1. When the negative class (non-progression) dominates, its high precision and recall will be averaged with the positive class's lower scores, inflating the apparent macro F1 relative to the minority-class F1 that actually matters clinically. AUROC or sensitivity-at-specificity would be more informative primary metrics for this setting.
defender_raw:
Predicting diabetic retinopathy progression—not just severity classification—is a significantly harder and more clinically valuable task than cross-sectional grading, and this design handles the longitudinal challenge carefully. The requirement for at least 12 months of follow-up before inclusion ensures that the binary progression label is observable for every patient in the cohort; without this constraint, censored cases would introduce selection bias. This is an important inclusion criterion that directly addresses a common failure mode in longitudinal imaging studies.

Using ophthalmologist-adjudicated ETDRS progression labels—blinded to baseline clinical data—is the correct labeling protocol. Blinding the adjudicators to clinical data prevents the baseline EHR features from influencing the outcome determination, which would introduce circular labeling bias. The adjudication process also ensures that borderline cases receive expert review rather than defaulting to a single rater's judgment.

Including imaging facility and follow-up interval as both stratification variables and model features is a nuanced and correct methodological decision. These variables are genuine confounds: facilities with better imaging quality may detect progression earlier, and longer follow-up intervals have higher progression probability by definition. By including them as features rather than removing them, the model can learn to correct for these effects rather than ignoring them—a more principled approach than trying to balance them away entirely.

The multi-modal architecture—ResNet-50 on retinal images combined with a three-layer MLP on tabular EHR features—tests the specific hypothesis that imaging adds predictive value beyond clinical data alone. The logistic regression baseline restricted to tabular features directly operationalizes this comparison. The macro-averaged F1-score primary metric is appropriate for a binary progression task with class imbalance: it weights false negatives (missed progressors) and false positives equally, which is the correct penalty structure when both under-treatment and over-treatment carry clinical costs.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split does not enforce patient-level exclusivity — patients with multiple time points may have encounters in both training and test sets
Preprocessing normalization fit on entire dataset before splitting reduces inter-facility distributional shift that would exist in real deployment
Ophthalmologist grading blinded to baseline EHR creates label-feature incoherence — labels reflect imaging-only adjudication while model uses clinical context for prediction
Macro-averaged F1 for binary classification inflates the apparent metric by averaging dominant negative-class performance with minority positive-class F1

Facility variation 'documented and retained as stratification variables' but facility-specific performance is only evaluated post-hoc, not used to validate generalization before reporting final results
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split does not enforce patient-level exclusivity — patients with multiple time points may have encounters in both training and test sets
Preprocessing normalization fit on entire dataset before splitting reduces inter-facility distributional shift that would exist in real deployment
